data.title: SL Green signals 2026 occupancy target of 94.8% as leasing momentum accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Marc Holliday, Interim President, Chairman & CEO, expressed strong confidence in New York City's fiscal stability and the commercial office sector's prospects, noting, "I think 2026 is setting up to be quite an amazing year for the commercial office sector in terms of occupancy gains, rental achievement and business growth." He highlighted robust business activity, citing "the big 5 banks just reported increases to earnings year-over-year with profits in the fourth quarter up 6.7%, and investment banking revenues up 12.6%." Holliday detailed a recent 10-day trip to Asia, reporting unprecedented global appetite for New York real estate: "The appetite to invest in New York was as strong as I have ever seen."

  • Holliday outlined ambitious plans for 2026, including a $7 billion refinancing initiative and a $2.5 billion asset disposition program, emphasizing, "We set lofty goals for ourselves in December, as we always do, and know you all will be monitoring our progress every step of the way, as you should."

  • Harrison Sitomer, Chief Investment Officer, shared that "we have seen a continued tightening of senior loans as demonstrated by our recent financing of Park Avenue Tower," and announced the launch of fundraising for a new fund focused on senior credit lending. Sitomer highlighted, "We are in negotiations on contracts and term sheets on 4 additional transactions in our $2.5 billion plan."

  • Matthew Diliberto, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "From an earnings perspective, we printed an FFO beat of $0.02 a share, driven by higher NOI due to lower expenses, net of reimbursements, which came through both in the earnings beat and in same-store cash NOI that was better than we expected for the quarter." Diliberto added, "We ended the year at 93% [occupancy], which is sector-leading and reflects an increase of almost 400 basis points since the lows at the end of the first quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • Diliberto reiterated confidence in the company's leasing trajectory, setting a 2026 same-store occupancy objective of 94.8% by year-end. Diliberto projected, "It translates into significant same-store NOI growth, 3.5% to 4.5% over the course of the year." Management indicated that a strong leasing pipeline and ongoing asset sales will support these targets.

  • No explicit comparison to analysts' estimates was provided due to a lack of sufficient data.

Financial Results

  • Diliberto highlighted an FFO beat of $0.02 per share for the fourth quarter, attributing the outperformance to higher NOI, improved hospitality business contributions, and lower G&A. He noted that the company "actually beat the initial guidance we gave back in December of 2024 by $65 million, almost $20 million of which happened in the fourth quarter alone."

  • The company closed nearly 800,000 square feet of Manhattan office leasing in the quarter, totaling 2.6 million square feet for the year, and achieved sector-leading occupancy. Diliberto stated, "Including the same-store leases that were signed after January 1 in our December occupancy, we would have been at 93.2%."

Q&A

  • Alexander Goldfarb, Piper Sandler: Asked about AI's impact on tenant demand. Steven Durels, Executive VP, responded that "I've not heard of a single instance of the deals that we've done where tenants have downsized as a result of AI. Just the opposite, many of the deals that we're working on... have some element of growth."

  • John Kim, BMO: Inquired about the timing of recognizing $78 million in rental revenue from economic vs. physical occupancy. Diliberto explained, "How it bleeds in somewhat out of our control because the tenants control when they finish their space and can move in, and that's what triggers revenue recognition."

  • Nicholas Yulico, Scotiabank: Asked about asset sales timing and dividend policy. Diliberto clarified most asset sales will impact the back half of the year, spanning "stabilized office, development sites, residential, and retail." On dividends, Holliday said, "We don't peg the policy quarter-to-quarter. It's intended to be underpinning of a long-term plan of investment, and harvesting, repatriation and creating free cash flow."

  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan: Queried about economic occupancy guidance and Worldwide Plaza's FFO impact. Diliberto confirmed year-end economic occupancy is expected to be higher and reported, "It generates $7 million of FFO."

  • Several analysts probed leasing pipeline quality, concessions trends, and capital deployment from the debt fund. Management described a strong leasing pipeline, stable concessions, and fund deployment targeting mid-teens returns.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the sustainability of earnings, timing of asset sales, dividend policy, and leasing economics, displaying a slightly skeptical to neutral tone, particularly regarding recurring profitability and dividend sustainability.

  • Management maintained an optimistic and confident tone throughout, frequently citing "a very high degree of confidence in executing on that plan" and repeatedly emphasizing sector leadership and growth prospects. During Q&A, management remained constructive but deflected on quarterly dividend specifics and timing of certain revenue streams, indicating a preference for long-term context.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, the current call featured a more assertive and bullish management tone, with analysts maintaining measured skepticism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from projecting occupancy at 93.2% year-end in Q3 to a new target of 94.8% by the end of 2026.

  • Strategic focus in Q4 centered on executing a $7 billion refinancing and $2.5 billion disposition plan, compared to Q3’s emphasis on acquisitions and new development sites.

  • Analysts in both quarters focused on asset sales, leasing momentum, dividend sustainability, and profitability metrics, but Q4 included more granular discussion of fee income, leasing pipeline composition, and international capital flows.

  • Management’s tone in Q4 was more confident, highlighting sector-leading occupancy gains and an improved capital markets environment, while Q3 focused more on navigating challenges and recent transaction activity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the unpredictability of FAD due to tenant capital spend and timing of asset sales, noting, "FAD is not the governor of dividend."

  • Analysts raised concerns about dividend sustainability, timing of asset sales, and the impact of delayed revenue recognition due to the difference between economic and physical occupancy.

  • Management emphasized a diversified revenue strategy, stating the dividend policy is based on "an accumulation of taxable income items," not just FAD or FFO metrics.

Final Takeaway

SL Green management reiterated optimism for 2026, underpinned by strong leasing activity, robust demand from both domestic and international investors, and sector-leading occupancy gains. The company aims to drive NOI growth and execute its refinancing and disposition plans, positioning itself for continued earnings momentum. Management stressed confidence in the city’s business climate and in their ability to deliver above-peer performance, emphasizing that the portfolio is at its highest earning capacity and poised for further opportunity and value growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold and silver end higher, but sudden midday selloff may indicate near-term peak
data.description:
Gold and silver futures settled slightly higher in a volatile session after gold retreated suddenly in late-morning trading from a record above $5,600/oz and silver slipped from a record $120/oz.

"We believe this reversal is significant," and it may indicate that precious metals prices have found a near-term high, Spartan Capital analyst Peter Cardillo said in a note.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for February delivery closed +0.3% to $5,318.40/oz, pulling back from an intraday record above $5,600/oz but nevertheless setting another new all-time high, and front-month February Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished +0.7% at $114.037/oz, its second-highest settlement in history.

Both metals started much higher in early trading on a weaker dollar, threats of U.S. military strikes on Iran, and a looming deadline for the U.S. government to avoid another shutdown.

Despite the day's sudden pullback, UBS analysts led by Giovanni Staunovo said gold remains an attractive asset and a strong hedge, forecasting the metal to reach $6,200 for the first three quarters of the year, driven by higher investment activity.

The World Gold Council said it expects global demand for gold to remain strong this year, driven by lower interest rates, uncertainty in bond markets, and persistent geopolitical risks.

"Gold's appeal as an all-weather hedge relative to fixed income should continue to attract material investor demand into 2026, and possibly beyond," WGC said in its quarterly report.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Cascades to sell Richmond, British Columbia packaging plant to Crown Paper Group
data.description:
• Cascades (CAS:CA) has entered into an agreement with Crown Paper Group for the sale of its corrugated packaging plant located in Richmond, British Columbia, for a total value of $65.5 million.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Vivian Health announces leadership changes; appoints Bill Kong CEO
data.description:
• Vivian Health, a subsidiary of IAC (IAC), announced on Thursday the appointment of Bill Kong to the role of CEO.
  • As CEO, Kong will spearhead the company's go-forward strategy as it continues building out its industry-leading talent marketplace, accelerating product innovation, and scaling through AI innovation.
  • The company said Kong succeeds co-founder Parth Bhakta as CEO, who will transition to executive chairman and partner closely with Mr. Kong and Vivian's executive management team to drive a new chapter of AI-driven growth and scale.
  • It added that the change also includes chief financial officer Adam Greenberg stepping into an expanded role as president & CFO, overseeing financial strategy, business, people and commercial operations, and corporate development. All changes are effective immediately.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Cullen/Frost outlines 5–7% loan growth and $300M buyback for 2026 amid continued expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cullen/Frost Bankers, Inc. (CFR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Phillip Green reported fourth quarter net income of $164.6 million, a 7.4% increase year-over-year, with earnings per share of $2.56, up 8.5% from the previous year. He stated, “Our organic expansion strategy continues to generate positive results. As of quarter end, expansion deposits exceeded $3 billion… expansion loans stood at $2.37 billion. In total, the expansion has added more than 78,000 new households.”
  • Green highlighted the consumer bank’s household growth of 5.8% for 2025 and mortgage lending platform surpassing its year-end goal, ending 2025 with $595 million in loans outstanding, “delivering our best quarter to date with an increase of $173 million in outstanding loans during the fourth quarter.”
  • Commercial business saw record outreach and new relationship growth, with 4,091 new commercial relationships in 2025, up 8% from 2024, and new loan commitments in Q4 rising 22% from Q3. Green noted, “Our overall credit quality remains good by historical standards with net charge-offs and nonperforming assets both at healthy levels.”
  • The company restructured its wealth management business for long-term organic growth and created better alignment between commercial banking and insurance brokerage segments.
  • CFO Dan Geddes stated, “Expansion locations delivered $0.12 of EPS accretion compared to $0.09 in the third quarter. This expansion EPS contribution for the quarter was driven by Houston 1.0, generating $0.15 per share with Houston 2.0 and Dallas now at breakeven, and Austin… costing $0.03 per share.”
  • Geddes confirmed the board’s approval of a new 1-year $300 million share repurchase program, following completion of the previous $150 million plan.

Outlook

  • The company projects net interest income growth of 3% to 5% for 2026. Geddes elaborated: “For net interest margin, we expect an improvement of about 5 to 10 basis points compared to our full year 2025 net interest margin of 3.66%.”
  • Full year average loan growth is expected in the range of 5% to 7% and average deposits up 2% to 3%. Noninterest income is projected to grow 4% to 5%, with noninterest expense growth in the 5% to 6% range. Geddes added, “We expect full year 2026 [net charge-offs] to be in a range of 20 to 25 basis points of average loans.”

Financial Results

  • The fourth quarter net interest margin was 3.66%, down from 3.69% last year. Average deposits rose to $43.3 billion and average loans to $21.7 billion, both higher year-over-year. The net unrealized loss on the available-for-sale portfolio decreased to $1.04 billion from $1.14 billion last quarter.
  • Noninterest income included a $5.4 million annual Visa bonus, while expenses reflected a $7.2 million increase in stock compensation, a $4.2 million one-time payroll transition expense, and a $4 million write-off related to technology modernization.

Q&A

  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked about credit quality and risk grade 10 loan migrations. Green explained that “no, there was no charge-off on the shared national credit. We did add a specific reserve of $10 million on it…they’re trying to work that out, and we’re optimistic that they’ll have some success.” Geddes noted a “nice churn” in risk grade 10 resolutions.
  • Shaw, Barclays: Queried deposit beta with rate cuts. Geddes replied, “I think it’d stay where it is…around that 43% of interest-bearing costs.”
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Questioned why loan and deposit growth guidance isn’t higher. Geddes cited accelerated payoffs in early 2026, with stronger loan growth expected in the second half. On deposits, “it’s a competitive environment…there’s still options out there off balance sheet.”
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Asked about timing for operating leverage improvement. Geddes discussed delayed recognition of loan commitments and expected consumer mortgage growth, with Green emphasizing market disruption as an opportunity for new relationships.
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: Inquired about fee income guidance and buyback pace. Geddes pointed to one-time income items and expects more consistent buybacks in 2026.
  • Peter Winter, D.A. Davidson: Examined fee income growth drivers. Green attributed it to account growth, while Geddes noted commercial service charges may rise with rate cuts.
  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: Explored pipeline strength. Geddes reported the pipeline is “up 16% this time last year,” particularly in commercial real estate, and highlighted expansion markets’ contribution.
  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC: Probed NII sensitivity to rate cuts. Geddes stated, “A cut is roughly $2 million a month to net interest income…if we don’t get the April cut, you’re looking at $16 million additional net interest income.”
  • Ben Gerlinger, Citi: Clarified that guidance is GAAP-based. Geddes confirmed this.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on whether guidance is conservative and sought clarity on the impact of accelerated payoffs, competitive pressures, buyback pace, and pipeline momentum, reflecting a neutral to slightly skeptical tone.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, highlighting durability of expansion results, credit quality, and market opportunities. Green’s remarks on competition and expansion were optimistic: “We are a low-cost producer as it relates to funding costs…we’re able to respond…very effectively against, say, a price competitor.”
  • Compared to last quarter, management’s tone remained confident but more focused on explaining guidance conservatism amid transitional loan dynamics and heightened competition. Analysts’ tone was similarly inquisitive as before but more focused on timing and magnitude of inflection points.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more subdued, with projected net interest income and loan growth slightly lower than last quarter’s expectations. Expense growth guidance was reduced to 5%–6%, down from 8%–9% previously.
  • Management’s confidence in expansion accretion strengthened, with EPS contribution rising from $0.09 to $0.12 quarter-over-quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from general credit and competition to timing of loan growth acceleration and operating leverage, while management’s responses centered on navigating competitive dynamics and leveraging disruption in Texas markets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management called out nonperforming assets rising to $72 million, with most of the increase tied to one borrower in beverage distribution, mitigated by a $10 million specific reserve. Multifamily credit migration and competitive deposit environment were highlighted as ongoing risks.
  • Expense growth includes technology investments and potential AI initiatives, though management expects these to be self-funding through efficiencies. Intensifying competition from new market entrants and possible headwinds from pricing were acknowledged.

Final Takeaway

Cullen/Frost Bankers, Inc. closed 2025 with robust organic expansion, stable credit quality, and meaningful deposit and loan growth. The company projects continued momentum in 2026, targeting 5% to 7% loan growth and 2% to 3% deposit growth, while executing a new $300 million share repurchase plan. Management remains confident in leveraging market disruption and competitive strengths, emphasizing long-term relationship building as the foundation for future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Invesco Mortgage Q4 earnings beat on Agency RMBS outperformance
data.description:
Invesco Mortgage Capital (IVR) on Thursday turned in a Q4 2025 profit that was bolstered by a combination of widening swap spreads and relative outperformance in agency residential mortgage-backed securities during the quarter.

Q4 earnings available for distribution per share of $0.56, meeting the average analyst estimate, slipped from $0.58 a year earlier.

Effective net interest income edged up to $47.7M in Q4 from $46.8M in the year-ago period, vs. $48.0M expected.

IVR shares inched up 0.2% in after-hours trading.

Total expenses were $4.57M, down from $4.78M in Q4 2024.

Book value per share of $8.72 at Dec. 31, 2025, rose to $8.41 at Sept. 30, 2025.

"Given the meaningful decline in interest rate volatility, we remain constructive on Agency RMBS, though we view near-term risks as balanced following the sector's strong performance, reinforced by the recent announcements that Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) will purchase $200 billion in Agency RMBS," CEO John Anzalone said in a statement.

Conference call on Jan. 30 at 9:00 a.m. ET.

data.title: U.S. Treasury adds Thailand to economies it's monitoring for currency practices
data.description:
In its semiannual report to Congress on macroeconomic and foreign exchange policies of major trading partners, the U.S. Department of the Treasury listed 10 economies that it's watching regarding their currency practices and macroeconomic policies, it said on Thursday.

China, Japan, Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, Singapore, Vietnam, Germany, Ireland, and Switzerland merit close attention, the Treasury Department said. Thailand was added to the list since its last report in June 2025.

While China wasn't designated a currency manipulator, the country "stands out among our major trading partners in its lack of transparency around its exchange rate policies and practices," the report said.

Treasury found that no major trading partner met all three criteria for enhanced analysis under the Trade Facilitation and Trade Enforcement Act of 2015 during the four quarters ending June 2025. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Honeywell outlines 6–9% EPS growth target for 2026 as portfolio transformation accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Honeywell International Inc. (HON) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chairman & CEO Vimal Kapur highlighted that "Honeywell delivered a strong fourth quarter to close 2025, exceeding our expectations for both adjusted sales and adjusted EPS with orders up 23%, driving our backlog to over $37 billion." He emphasized the company's conviction in "another year of meaningful top and bottom-line growth of 2026," supported by portfolio actions, innovation from R&D investment, and record backlog.
  • Kapur announced the completion of the Solstice Advanced Materials spin and the expectation to complete the Aerospace spin in the third quarter of 2026. He detailed new leadership for Aerospace, including Jim Currier as President and CEO, Josh Jepsen as CFO, and Craig Arnold as Non-Executive Chair of the Board. Indra Nooyi was also appointed to Honeywell's Board of Directors.
  • The company reorganized its segments into Aerospace Technologies, Building Automation, Process Automation and Technology, and Industrial Automation, aiming for a simplified and synergetic model. A sale of Productivity Solutions and Services and Warehouse and Workflow Solutions is planned for the first half of 2026.
  • Kapur underscored Quantinuum’s progress, including raising approximately $840 million at a $10 billion valuation, launching the Helios quantum computer, and establishing partnerships with NVIDIA, JPMorgan, Amgen, and Mitsui.
  • Senior VP & CFO Mike Stepniak stated, "Sales grew 11% organically or 6% excluding the impact of the 2024 Bombardier agreement, led by double-digit growth in aerospace and high single-digit growth in building automation." He added that free cash flow for the quarter was $2.5 billion, up 48%.

Outlook

  • For 2026, Honeywell anticipates sales of $38.8 billion to $39.8 billion, up 3% to 6% organically, with adjusted EPS of $10.35 to $10.65, representing 6% to 9% growth. Stepniak stated, "We expect growth to be led by aerospace on higher commercial demand and increased defense budgets and building automation driven by new product innovations."
  • Segment margins are expected to increase 20 to 60 basis points to 22.7% to 23.1%, supported by price execution, productivity, and cost reduction. Free cash flow is expected to reach $5.3 billion to $5.6 billion, up 4% to 10%.
  • The Aerospace spin is anticipated for Q3 2026, with both Aerospace and Automation hosting Investor Days in June.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter adjusted earnings per share were $2.59, up 17%. Full-year adjusted EPS was $9.78, up 12%. Free cash flow for the year was $5.1 billion, up 20%.
  • Segment profit increased 23% for the quarter, with margins of 22.8%. Aerospace segment margin expanded to 26.5% sequentially, while Building Automation margins reached 27%.
  • Orders grew 23% organically, pushing backlog up 15%.

Q&A

  • Julian Mitchell, Barclays: Asked about margin progression and segment drivers. Stepniak responded, "Operationally, we really are expanding margins about 50 to 90 basis points...Second half looks much better than the first half."
  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe Research: Queried about Quantinuum investment and IPO plans. Stepniak confirmed a $100 million year-over-year increase in investment, stating, "We fully consolidate Quantinuum right now."
  • Scott Davis, Melius Research: Asked about pricing strategy. Kapur explained, "Our pricing strategy...has become more mature really to look at it as a long-term trend, work with our customers and align with them."
  • C. Stephen Tusa, JPMorgan: Probed on volume and stranded costs. Stepniak said, "Price will be above 3%...on average should be 3.5%." Kapur added, "We have already neutralized the Advanced Materials stranded costs in 2026."
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Focused on Aerospace end market growth and investments. Kapur indicated Defense and Space would lead with high single-digit growth, and investments are increasing, particularly in Aero supply chain capacity.
  • Amit Mehrotra, UBS: Inquired about recurring revenue in Building Automation. Kapur stated, "We have been working very hard to change our business model to more recurring revenue...We do expect that to become an enabler for recurring revenue growth in the process segment in the near future."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was inquisitive and occasionally skeptical, especially around margin progression, pricing sustainability, and portfolio action timelines. Questions often pressed for detail on headwinds, investment levels, and strategic execution.
  • Management's tone was confident, emphasizing clarity and conviction in guidance and strategic execution. Kapur used phrases like "We are very confident" and "We remain very confident" when addressing margin expansion and portfolio moves.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts showed increased focus on strategic execution, particularly regarding the separation milestones and Quantinuum.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from an emphasis on 2025 outperformance to detailed forward-looking growth targets and margin expansion for 2026.
  • The current quarter featured more explicit discussion of the impact and timing of the Aerospace spin, segment realignment, and sales processes for divested units.
  • Management reinforced confidence in pricing as a durable driver, whereas the previous quarter highlighted some lag effects and transitionary pricing headwinds.
  • Analysts continued to focus on margin sustainability, pricing, and the impact of structural portfolio changes, but questioned recurring revenue and R&D conversion more than previously.
  • Key metrics such as backlog and order growth remained in focus, with management noting stronger momentum and visibility for 2026.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted continued pressure on catalyst demand in petrochemicals and short-cycle automation, with project deferrals remaining a challenge.
  • Regional weakness in Industrial Automation was noted, especially in China and Europe. Kapur stated, "segments of IA business in China and in Europe, the exposures we have in end markets we serve, we see pressure there."
  • Increased R&D and Quantinuum investments present a margin headwind for 2026.
  • The company is tracking slow conversion of catalyst orders and remains cautious on short-cycle demand recovery.

Final Takeaway

Honeywell management expressed strong confidence in the company's trajectory for 2026, citing successful portfolio actions, robust order growth, and continued innovation as key drivers. The anticipated Aerospace spin, new segment structure, and investments in quantum computing position the company for incremental value creation, with management maintaining conviction in achieving its new growth and margin targets amid ongoing market transformation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
